coredata.dc:title: Modulation of carbon dots hybrids lasers for high security flexible multi-level anti-counterfeiting
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Shaofeng
Wang, Jingjing
Ni, Yiqun
Li, Yunfei
Zhang, Hailong
Zhang, Wenfei
Xie, Zheng
Zhou, Shuyun
coredata.dc:description: Anti-counterfeiting materials development has been raised for a long time to improve the level of security against counterfeiting. Increasing counterfeiting difficulties as well as reducing difficulties of materials preparation are two major concerns in the development of anti-counterfeiting materials. Herein, carbon dots (CDs) and triethyl 1,3,5-benzenetricarboxylate (Et3BTC), both of which show two random lasing peaks located around 325 nm and 450 nm at 265 nm excitation are chosen as two lasing mediums to form hybrids. Moreover, lasing intensity ratio of the two lasing bands from the hybrids can be tuned by changing the working temperature, excitation area, and even bending angle of substrate. As a result, a strategy of dual random lasing medium modulation based high security flexible multi-level anti-counterfeiting technique is developed. Thus, it is suitable for anti-counterfeiting of high-value items due to its high security and excellent waterproofness. This work will provide a perspective to boost the development of high security anti-counterfeiting techniques.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Flexible multi-level anti-counterfeiting
Random lasing
Dual lasing medium modulation
Banknotes

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast footnotes grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst pubtype ref specialabst

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a-b) TEM image. (c-d) HRTEM image, both of the scale bars are 2 nm. (e) The particle sizes distributions of CDs. (f) XRD patterns of CDs. (g) 1H NMR spectrum of CDs. (h) FTIR spectrum of CDs.

(a) XPS spectrum of CDs. High-resolution XPS spectra corresponding to C1 s (b), O1 s (c), and Si2 p (d). (e) The UV-Vis spectrum of CDs and the emission spectra of CDs, Et3BTC, and CDs/Et3BTC hybrids. The inset of Figure (e) is the TEM image of CDs/Et3BTC hybrids. (f) The relationship between F450/F325 and pump power of CDs and Et3BTC. (g) Stability of photoluminescence of CDs/Et3BTC hybrids, the insets are the photos of CDs solution under daylight (left) and UV lamp (right).

Variation of F450/F325 with (a) temperature and (b) excitation area. (c) Schematic of encoding technique with the hybrids. (d) Photographs of the hybrids patterns blended with the fabric under ambient illumination (the region of the orange dotted line). (e) Luminescent photographs of the encoded “01010011″, “01011010″, and “01010101″ under 265 nm excitation, which can be translated into “SZU”. Micrograph of the hybrids blended with the fabric under (f) ambient illumination and (g) 265 nm excitation.

(a) Schematic of optical setup of the flexible anti-counterfeiting. Micrograph of the hybrids blended within Egyptian Pound under (b) ambient illumination, (c) 265 nm excitation. (d) Lasing spectra of CDs/Et3BTC hybrids blended within Egyptian Pound under different working temperatures. (e) Variation of F450/F325 with temperature. (f) Lasing spectra of CDs/Et3BTC hybrids blended within Egyptian Pound under different bending angles. (g) Variation of F450/F325 with bending angle. (h) Lasing spectra of CDs/Et3BTC hybrids blended within Egyptian Pound under different excitation area. (i) Variation of F450/F325 with excitation area.
(a) Lasing spectra, (b-d) Photographs of CDs/Et3BTC hybrids blended within Egyptian Pound before wetting, after wetting, and after drying respectively.

head.ce:title: Modulation of carbon dots hybrids lasers for high security flexible multi-level anti-counterfeiting

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shenzhen Key Laboratory of Laser Engineering, College of Physics and Optoelectronic Engineering, Shenzhen University, Shenzhen 518060, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shenzhen Key Laboratory of Laser Engineering, College of Physics and Optoelectronic Engineering, Shenzhen University

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Photochemical Conversion and Optoelectronic Materials, Technical Institute of Physics and Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Beijing 100190, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Photochemical Conversion and Optoelectronic Materials, Technical Institute of Physics and Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory for Advanced Metals and Materials, University of Science and Technology Beijing, Beijing 100083, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory for Advanced Metals and Materials, University of Science and Technology Beijing

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory for Advanced Metals and Materials, University of Science and Technology Beijing, Beijing 100083, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Anti-counterfeiting materials development has been raised for a long time to improve the level of security against counterfeiting. Increasing counterfeiting difficulties as well as reducing difficulties of materials preparation are two major concerns in the development of anti-counterfeiting materials. Herein, carbon dots (CDs) and triethyl 1,3,5-benzenetricarboxylate (Et3BTC), both of which show two random lasing peaks located around 325 nm and 450 nm at 265 nm excitation are chosen as two lasing mediums to form hybrids. Moreover, lasing intensity ratio of the two lasing bands from the hybrids can be tuned by changing the working temperature, excitation area, and even bending angle of substrate. As a result, a strategy of dual random lasing medium modulation based high security flexible multi-level anti-counterfeiting technique is developed. Thus, it is suitable for anti-counterfeiting of high-value items due to its high security and excellent waterproofness. This work will provide a perspective to boost the development of high security anti-counterfeiting techniques.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A strategy of high security flexible multi-level anti-counterfeiting technique is developed by modulation of lasing at UV-B (325 nm) and visible (450 nm) regimes from carbon dots hybrids. This development is suitable for anti-counterfeiting on high-value items due to its high security and excellent waterproofness.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: During globalization, counterfeit products have long been a challenge [1,2]. In the past decades, spontaneous emission, such as fluorescence, phosphorous, and up-conversion emission, has been utilized in majority of anti-counterfeiting technologies due to the abundance of materials that can produce spontaneous emission [3–8]. The high accessibility of spontaneous emission lowers down the application barriers of anti-counterfeiting on the one hand, while on the other hand makes it easy to counterfeit. By comparison, lasing, which requires specific conditions to generate, is suitable to be used in high security requirement areas [9–12]. Among all lasing wavelengths, lasing at the ultraviolet B (UV-B) band is able to provide a much higher level of security against counterfeiting due to the high requirement of optical gain to balance optical loss.

Normally, fluorescent materials contain only one type of luminescence medium [13–17]. Fabrication of composites involving multiple luminescence mediums is aimed to add the new emission peaks [18–20], to enhance the fluorescence intensity [21], to regulate the optical properties [22], or to improve physical properties [23], which has been widely used in radiometric fluorescence probe [24,25], anti-counterfeiting [20,26], etc. It is well known that the commonly used anti-counterfeiting luminescent labels are generally constituted from a single type of material, and have the advantages of convenient use and simple preparation, but also exhibit the single emission mode simultaneously, which makes them easily imitated [5,6]. By comparison, materials involving multiple emission modes are given more complex emission behaviors [27–30]. Furthermore, a combination of multiple luminescence mediums and lasing under flexible behavior provides a much higher level of security against counterfeiting [31].

In this work, CDs (carbon dots) and Et3BTC (triethyl 1,3,5-benzenetricarboxylate) are used as two gain mediums to achieve lasing at UV-B (325 nm) and visible (450 nm) regimes, respectively. Responses (i. e. intensity ratio of the two peaks) of the two random lasing mediums (i. e. CDs and Et3BTC) under different lasing conditions are found to be completely distinct. Therefore, intensity of the two lasing peaks can be tuned by changing the working temperature, the excitation area and even the bending angle of substrate. Finally, we proposed a strategy of dual random lasing medium modulation based multi-level anti-counterfeiting technique combined with a flexible substrate, which is suitable for varieties of non-woven fabric products, like banknotes. This work provides a significant guidance to promote further development in high security flexible anti-counterfeiting.

Triethoxyvinylsilane (KH151) was purchased from InnoChem Science & Technology Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). 1,3,5-benzenetricarboxylic acid trimethyl ester (Et3BTC) was purchased from Thermo Fisher Scientific Inc. (Shanghai, China). Ethanol (EtOH) was purchased from Modern Oriental Technology Development Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). In addition, the raw materials mentioned above were used directly without any purification.

The CDs were prepared by solvothermal method. In particular, 7 ml of Triethoxyvinylsilane (KH151) was added into 10 ml of ethanol, which was mixed under magnetic stirring for 2 min. This solution was then transferred to an 80 ml Teflon lined autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 12 h. After cooling to room temperature, the supernatant was collected and added to the dialysis bag (MWCO: 1k D). The purification process is maintained for 3 days with the ethanol as the exchange solvent. The CDs were obtained from the inside of dialysis bag [32].

In a typical process, 192 mg of CDs ethanol solution (270 mg/ml) was mixed with 35 mg of Et3BTC and subsequently heated at 125 ℃ to form the transparent liquid hybrids. After that, the liquid was dripped onto substrates/fabrics/banknotes and cooled down to room temperature naturally. By this method, CDs/Et3BTC hybrids were formed on the substrates or blended with fabrics or banknotes.

Morphology of the CDs was conducted by using a JEM-2100 F high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM, Hitachi, Japan) and an FEI Tecnai G2 F30 high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM, FEI, America). Chemical compositions of CDs were analyzed by a Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR, Excalibur 3100, Varian, USA) and an X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Axis Ultra DLD, Kratos, USA). The phase identification was measured by a D8 focus X-ray diffractometer (XRD, Bruker, German). The NMR spectrum was obtained on an Avance-400 nuclear magnetic resonance spectrometer (Bruker, German). Neodymium-doped yttrium aluminum garnet (Nd:YAG) pulsed laser (355 nm, 6 ns, 10 Hz, Continuum Surelite, California, USA) with an optical parameter oscillator (Continuum Horizon, California, USA) acted as an excitation source for the lasing measurements. Ultraviolet-Visible spectrophotometry (UV-Vis, U-3000, Hamamatsu Photonics, Japan) and fluorescence spectrometer (PL, Edinburgh Instruments FS5, UK) were used to detect the optical properties of CDs. Laser spectra were characterized by a HORIBA iHR320 spectrophotometer (Minami-ku, Kyoto, Japan).

As shown in the transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images ( Fig. 1a and b), CDs, which are of quasi-spherical shape, are randomly dispersed without aggregation. The average size of CDs is approximately 3.5 nm ranging from 2.0 nm to 5.5 nm (Fig. 1e). HRTEM images show that the lattice spacing of CDs is 0.21 nm, corresponding to the (100) planes of graphite (Fig. 1c and d) [33]. Fig. 1 f shows the XRD spectrum of CDs with a broad peak located at 2θ = 23.1°, corresponding to a lattice distance of 0.39 nm, which relates to the graphene (002) planes from the carbon core of the CDs [34]. A sharp diffraction peak emerging at 2θ = 9.3° is also observed, corresponding to a lattice distance of 0.94 nm, which is larger than that of graphene oxide (0.75 nm). This is due to the enlargement of the lattice distance of CDs with different oxidation degrees. Fig. 1 g plots the 1H NMR spectrum of CDs. The chemical shifts indicate that CH2 =CH2 (δ = 6.01), CH2 (δ = 3.74) and CH3 (δ = 1.25) are observed on CDs. This result is in accordance with FTIR analysis (Fig. 1 h), in which the stretching vibrations of -OH/C-H (at the end of the alkene) at 3420 cm−1 and 3060 cm−1 are observed. The absorption bands located at 2975 cm−1, 1600 cm−1, and 1407 cm−1 are assigned to -CH3 stretching vibrations, CH=CH2 stretching vibrations, and CH=CH2 deformation vibrations respectively. It also gives a typical signal arising from the Si-O-C at 1080 cm−1.

The FTIR spectrum can be further supported by the XPS analysis. As expected, the complete XPS scan, in Fig. 2a, shows the CDs containing C, O, and Si with relative abundances of 40.13%, 34.56%, and 20.73%, respectively. The high resolution C1 s spectrum is fitted into three peaks located at 284.2 eV, 284.9 eV, and 285.8 eV, which are ascribed to CH=CH2, Si-O-C, and COH bonds respectively (Fig. 2b). The O1 s spectrum presents three peaks located at 532.2 eV, 532.8 eV, and 533.8 eV, which are related to Si-O-C, COH, and Si-O-Si bonds respectively (Fig. 2c). Finally, the Si2p spectrum exhibits three main peaks located at 101.5 eV, 102.6 eV, and 103.4 eV, which are corresponding to Si-CH=CH2, Si-O-C, and Si-O-Si bonds, respectively (Fig. 2d).

CDs are dispersed in Et3BTC to form CDs/Et3BTC hybrids. The TEM image of the hybrids in the inset of Fig. 2e reveals that CDs are homogeneously dispersed in Et3BTC without change in morphology. UV-Vis spectrum of CDs is shown in Fig. 2e, in which the absorption peak located between 260 nm and 270 nm is attributed to the π-π * transition of CC bond [3]. Fig. 2e also plots the photoluminescence spectra of the CDs, Et3BTC, and CDs/Et3BTC hybrids at 265 nm excitation. From Fig. 2e, the broad emission spectrum of CDs with dual emissive peaks located around 325 nm and 450 nm (i.e., bluish white emission as shown in photograph of the CDs illuminated under a UV lamp in the inset of Fig. 2g) is observed. It is noted that the spectrum of Et3BTC is similar to the CDs with a narrower spectral width and relatively obvious deep ultraviolet peak. As a result, the spectrum of CDs/Et3BTC hybrids is comprised of spectra of both CDs and Et3BTC as shown in Fig. 2e. Moreover, it is found in Fig. 2f that the variations of lasing intensity ratio of the two peaks (F450/F325) from CDs and Et3BTC with different pump power are completely distinct. Simultaneously, superior emission stability is observed from the two peaks. As shown in Fig. 2g, emission intensity of the two peaks retains 97% of the original value, and the F450/F325 maintains stable after storage in the ambient environment over 8 months. Simultaneously, shapes of the emission spectra remains unchanged (Fig. S1).

As a result, lasing behavior can be tuned by changing the lasing conditions. As shown in Fig. 3a, the intensity ratio of the two peaks (i.e., F450/F325) increases by increasing temperature until the temperature reaches 90 ℃. This results from a much quicker decline of optical gain at 325 nm than that at 450 nm. However, the decrease of F450/F325 begins to occur when the temperature is higher than 90 ℃. This is attributed to insufficient optical gain which is not high enough to support lasing at 325 nm [12], leading to spontaneous emission without laser. Hence, the intensity at 450 nm declines much quicker than that at 325 nm at this stage. This can be verified by lasing spectra of hybrids patterns at different temperatures as shown in Fig. S2. It is noted that the lasing peak at 325 nm decreases with increasing temperature, and disappears at temperature higher than 90 ℃. In the meanwhile, to verify that the hybrids feature fabulous adaptability to the surface of multiple materials, we tested the performance of the hybrids absorbed in two other typical substrates (i.e., paper and cloth). Similar results can be observed in both two materials (Fig. S3a-d). Before the temperature reaches 90 ℃, F450/F325 increases by increasing temperature. After that, the decrease of F450/F325 begins to occur when the temperature is higher than 90 ℃. This indicates that lasing behavior of the hybrids is independent on the supporting substrates.

Moreover, F450/F325 can be tuned by altering the excitation area. As shown in Figure 3b, F450/F325 initially increases, and then decreases with an inflection point at 2 mm2 of excitation area. This is due to the excitation area playing an important role in the path length traveled through the amplifying medium, which contributes to the total amount of gain in random lasing [35,36]. The optical gain becomes higher when the pump area is enlarged. When the pump area is small, lasing at 450 nm strengthens far more than that at 325 nm, which requires a higher optical gain than lasing at 450 nm. After the pump area reaches 2 mm2, however, the optical gain of lasing at 450 nm tends to saturate while the optical gain of lasing at 325 nm gets higher continually, leading to the decrease of F450/F325. This can be verified by the spectra of the hybrids as shown in Fig. S4.

Bestowed with their unique emission spectra and superior emission stability, the hybrids can find wide applications in varieties of areas. Optical anti-counterfeiting, which requires inimitable emission spectra along with long time stability is one of the most important applications. Hence, we proposed a strategy of CDs/Et3BTC hybrids based multi-level anti-counterfeiting technique. Fig. 3c is a schematic of the optical anti-counterfeiting coding. As shown in Fig. 3c, hybrids patterns are formed on a white substrate. Blue lasing patterns appear on the substrate under ultraviolet laser illumination. This feature suggests a way of optical encoding with the apparent difference of lasing or not lasing. It’s convenient to define the “1” and the “0”. While “1” is defined as the random lasing region, “0” is defined as the place that provides pure spontaneous emission, as shown in the schematic diagram in Fig. 3c. The application of this optical coding technology on non-woven fabric is shown in Fig. 3d and e. It is observed from Fig. 3d that, the hybrids are almost invisible on the surface of the non-woven fabric. This is because the hybrids blend with the non-woven fabric without changing the appearance of the fabric (Fig. 3 f). However, blue white light can be observed from the hybrids other than the fabrics under 265 nm excitation in Fig. 3 g. As a result, blue white lasing patterns emerge on the non-woven fabric immediately by 265 nm excitation presenting the binary encoding “01010011″ (S), “01011010″ (Z), “01010101″ (U), the abbreviation of “Shenzhen University” (Fig. 3e).

To broaden the application of the hybrids, multi-level anti-counterfeiting strategy, which does not affect the flexibility of the fabric, was developed on flexible textile. Fig. 4b and c show the photos of hybrids blended inside the Egyptian Pound (Egyptian currency) with and without laser irradiating. Starting with the first level of anti-counterfeiting, F450/F325 increases by increasing temperature initially, and the decrease of F450/F325 begins to occur when the temperature is higher than 90 ℃ (Fig. 4d and e). The second level of anti-counterfeiting, which can be applied to the flexible textile, is built on the relationship between F450/F325 and the bending angle of the banknotes. As shown in Fig. 4a, the hybrids are excited by a laser strip. To eliminate the effect of excitation area on optical gain, the length of the laser spot is sufficient to cover the area of hybrids blended within the banknotes. When the banknotes are bent in a certain angle, the length of the laser spot would be shortened without changing the total laser energy, leading to higher pump power. It is noted that the effect of changing the bending angle on F450/F325 is attributed to the change of pump power. This is result from two luminescent mediums of the CDs/Et3BTC hybrids. When the pump power is low, the main contribution to stimulated radiation is given by CDs. Fig. S5a shows the spectra of CDs with increasing the pump power. It is observed that the F450/F325 increases by increasing the pump power, which complies with the trend of the F450/F325 of CDs/Et3BTC hybrids when the pump power is low in Fig. S5a. However, Et3BTC plays a leading role in contributing to stimulated emission when the pump power exceeds a certain value, which causes the relatively quicker increase of the emission at 325 nm. As a result, the value of F450/F325 declines, complying with the trend of the F450/F325 of Et3BTC with increasing the pump power (Fig. S5b). While increasing the bending degree of the banknotes, the pump power of excitation increases accordingly. And when the angle exceeds 20°, the ratio begins to decrease with further increasing the angle (Fig. 4 f and g). Finally, the third level of anti-counterfeiting is shown in Fig. 4 h and i. Before the excitation area reaches 1.2 mm2, F450/F325 increases by enlarging the excitation area. And then, the decrease of F450/F325 begins to occur when the excitation area is larger than 1.2 mm2. To verify stability of this anti-counterfeiting, long-term high-power laser excitation is conducted. As shown in Fig. S6 that both I/I0 and F450/F325 stay stable under 100,000 pulses laser excitation. (Laser peak power: 310 kW/cm−2).

The waterproofness of the hybrids is one of the most important considerations in practical application. In this demonstration, an Egyptian Pound and a piece of non-woven fabric with hybrids had been immersed in water for ten minutes and were dried naturally afterward. Fig. 5 and S7 show photographs and emission spectra of the hybrids under 265 nm excitation before and after immersed in water when the hybrids are blended with banknotes and fabrics respectively. As shown in Fig. 5 and S7, no significant change is observed from both the photographs and emission spectra. To eliminate the protection of the currency, front and back side of the currency are tore off and the sandwiched carbon dots hybrids are exposed to water directly. As shown in Fig. S8, the lasing spectra and photographs were collected directly from carbon dots hybrids exposed to water. As expected, no significant changes are observed from both the photographs and emission spectra. This is a direct proof of excellent water proofing of the carbon dots hybrids. This means that the carbon dots hybrids have excellent waterproofness that could be used in harsh conditions such as rain, sweat, etc.

In summary, CDs and Et3BTC are used as two lasing gain mediums to form the hybrids. The hybrids can be easily blended with different kinds of substrates. Random lasing bands located around 325 nm and 450 nm are observed from the hybrids. Moreover, through changing the working temperature, the excitation area, and the bending angle of the substrate, the intensity of the two lasing bands can be tuned regularly. Hence, we proposed a strategy of dual random lasing medium modulation based flexible multi-level anti-counterfeiting technique and verified excellent stability even after aging for 8 months. The proposed strategy shows a highly advanced security with difficult duplication. As a result, this may boost the development of high security anti-counterfeiting techniques, which is expected to have a variety of applications in high-value banknotes, artworks, commodities, etc.

Shaofeng Zhang: Investigation, Methodology, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Jingjing Wang: Investigation, Methodology, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Yiqun Ni: Resources, Formal analysis. Yunfei Li: Writing – review & editing. Hailong Zhang: Funding acquisition, Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Wenfei Zhang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Zheng Xie: Conceptualization, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Shuyun Zhou: Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (61875127, 21875267, 51871014).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jallcom.2023.170307.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Transformation of random lasing to Fabry-Perot lasing: observation of high temperature lasing from carbon dots
